flashcards.front: What is a friend function in C++?
flashcards.back: A friend function is a non-member function that has access to private and protected members of a class. It is declared using the 'friend' keyword inside the class definition.

flashcards.front: Why use friend function for operator overloading?
flashcards.back: Friend functions allow symmetric treatment of operands. Both 'a + b' and 'b + a' work correctly when + is overloaded using a friend function, unlike member functions where only 'a + b' works.

flashcards.front: Syntax for overloading binary operator using friend function
flashcards.back: friend returnType operator+(const ClassName& obj1, const ClassName& obj2); - This is declared inside the class and defined outside.

flashcards.base: Yes, friend functions cannot be virtual. Virtual functions are related to inheritance and polymorphism, which applies to member functions only.

flashcards.front: Why return by reference in operator overloading?
flashcards.back: Returning by reference allows chained operations like 'a + b + c' and avoids unnecessary copying, improving efficiency.

flashcards.front: Operators that cannot be overloaded in C++
flashcards.back: Scope resolution operator (::), member access (.), pointer-to-member (.*), conditional (?:), and sizeof operator cannot be overloaded.

flashcards.front: Difference between member function and friend function overloading
flashcards.back: Member function uses 'this' pointer implicitly; friend function has two explicit parameters. Member function cannot overload << and >> for stream extraction/insertion when used as member.
friend ostream& operator<<(ostream& out, const ClassName& obj); - Returns ostream reference to allow chaining.
friend istream& operator>>(istream& in, ClassName& obj); - Returns istream reference and uses non-const reference for input object.
No, friend functions are not members of the class, so they don't have a 'this' pointer. They receive all objects as explicit parameters.

flashcards.front: What is operator symmetry in C++?
flashcards.back: Operator symmetry means 'a + b' and 'b + a' should give the same result. Friend functions ensure this by treating both operands equally.

flashcards.front: When to prefer friend function over member function?
flashcards.back: Prefer friend function when: (1) operand symmetry is needed, (2) overloading << or >> for streams, (3) when left operand is not a class object (e.g., 5 + obj).
